name: Cost Curves Chosen by 94% of Business People Surveyed

book_quotes.quote: A second type of study takes a very different tack. Eiteman and Guthrie (1952, 832–836) ask business people to consider eight charts depicting three types of average cost curves: rising costs (charts 1–2); broadly U-shaped curves in which costs fall to a minimum and then rise significantly until capacity (charts 3–5); curves in which costs decline steadily until a point at or near capacity (charts 6–7); and a curve in which average costs decline at first but are essentially flat for most levels of output (chart 8).
Figure 4.23 Cost Curves Chosen by 94% of Business People Surveyed Source: Eiteman and Guthrie 1952, 835.

When classified by product, there were 1,082 responses, of which 94% opted for the steadily declining-cost curves depicted in figure 4.23 below, while only 5.7% chose the U-shaped curves commonly assumed in standard textbooks. Counting the two respondents who chose flat cost curves, 94.3% of the business people surveyed contradicted the fundamental postulate of neoclassical theory on cost curves (836–838, table 3).

primary_source.agency: Wilford J. Eiteman and Glenn E. Guthrie (authors); American Economic Review (publisher)
primary_source.publication: Eiteman, W. J., & Guthrie, G. E. (1952). 'The Shape of the Average Cost Curve.' American Economic Review, 42(5), 832-838.
primary_source.table_or_series_id: Eiteman & Guthrie (1952), Table 3 (response distribution across 8 chart options, n = 1,082 product-responses); curves 6 and 7 reproduced in Shaikh fig. 4.23
primary_source.units: percentage of respondents (94% chose curves 6 or 7); curves themselves are conceptual cost vs. scale-of-output schematics
primary_source.license: JSTOR access; AER 1952 copyright; reproduced under fair use
primary_source.available_through: JSTOR (https://www.jstor.org/stable/1812527); American Economic Association journal archive

methodology_notes: S408 is a cross-sectional snapshot of one survey (Eiteman & Guthrie 1952). Content_type set to 'cross_sectional' (not time_series).

What Fig 4.23 actually reproduces: two of the eight cost-curve charts shown to survey respondents (charts 6 and 7), which 94% of business people chose as best describing their firm's actual cost structure. Each chart is a conceptual schematic showing unit cost on the y-axis and scale of output on the x-axis, with a vertical 'Capacity' line at the right edge.

Shaikh uses this as empirical evidence against neoclassical U-shaped cost curves and in support of the classical/post-Keynesian view that average cost typically falls smoothly until engineering capacity (book pp. 163-164).

Note Shaikh's caveat (p. 164): the Eiteman & Guthrie survey did not allow for multiple shifts, which is why the chosen curves look like single-shift segments rather than the full multi-shift spiky pattern derived in S401-S407.

extension_candidates intentionally empty: one-shot 1952 survey, not a recurring data series. The 'extension' for this empirical finding is the literature-review summary in Miller (2000, 121-122, 125-126) which Shaikh cites - documenting that Bain 1948, Johnston 1960, Walters 1963, Dean 1976, Mansfield 1988, Kahn 1989, Lavoie 1992 all reach similar conclusions (book footnote 36, p. 164).
open_questions: JSTOR URL stable/1812527 to be confirmed: Eiteman & Guthrie 1952 AER vol. 42 no. 5 is the citation; the stable ID above is provisional and should be verified before Phase 4 sign-off.

If RSCD framework treats this as a 'data series' for replication, the chart values themselves (unit cost vs. scale) are conceptual not numerical - i.e., there is no numerical dataset to register, only a qualitative survey-distribution percentage (94%) and the two reproduced chart shapes.
Phase 4 may want to add a 'qualitative_finding' content_type or sub-category for evidence like this.

review_history.notes: 1952 survey from Eiteman & Guthrie. Cross-sectional, not time series. JSTOR citation provisional.
